risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used permuted block randomization at the level of the patient. Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process using permuted blocks and a computer-generated assignment sequence, which is sufficient to judge truly random sequence generation as low risk.

Quote: "Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: This explicitly describes use of a sealed envelope opened only after enrollment/baseline assessment, which indicates the recruiter could not foresee the next assignment before the participant was enrolled.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." Comment: Blinding was only maintained up to the baseline interview and then necessarily broken because the intervention (viewing a decision-aid video and/or motivational interviewing versus booklet) made participants aware of their assignment. Because the main outcomes included subjective measures such as willingness to consider surgery and discussion with a PCP, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so the study is at high risk of performance bias across the outcome set overall.

Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." and "Participant interviews were completed at baseline and at 1, 3 & 12 months following the intervention... The willingness rating is a 5-category ordinal response scale... The Knee Expectations scale..." Comment: Outcomes were primarily assessed by patient self-report in follow-up interviews, so the patient was the outcome assessor. Because participants were explicitly not blinded after the intervention, knowledge of allocation could influence subjective measurements such as willingness and expectations. The study also reported discussion with PCP by telephone survey, which is still participant-reported and therefore vulnerable to detection bias. Although referral/orthopedic appointment were also validated by chart review, the domain must be judged at the level of the most vulnerable outcome, and the self-reported subjective outcomes make overall detection bias high.

Quote: "There were no losses to follow-up except for one patient in the MI arm and one patient in DA/MI arm. Over the course of the study 93% of the subjects completed at least 2 of the 3 follow-up interviews with no differences among the 4 intervention groups (p=0.62)." Comment: Attrition was extremely low and balanced across arms (only 2 total follow-up losses among 639 randomized, with one in MI and one in DA+MI). This is well below the strict thresholds for concern, and the paper also reports no between-group difference in follow-up completion. Although 24 patients were excluded from the final analysis because they received baseline assessment but never the intervention, the follow-up outcome missingness reported for analyzed participants is minimal and does not suggest attrition bias for the measured outcomes.
